title: First Steps on Mercury
narratorText: We have touched down on Mercury for the first time. The surface is alien — ancient, scarred, and brutally hot — but it is ours to begin understanding. The real work starts now.

title: The Inner Worlds Open
narratorText: Mercury's industry has matured enough to carry our ambitions outward. Mars and Venus are no longer distant questions; they are neighbouring futures we can begin to answer.

title: The First Days
narratorText: We have been on Mercury long enough now that the crew has stopped calling it an expedition and started calling it home. That shift — small as it sounds — changes everything about how we plan.

title: The Fire We Have Always Wanted
narratorText: We have cracked the last theoretical barrier. Deuterium fusion is no longer a dream on a whiteboard — it is a blueprint waiting for the infrastructure to give it form. The energy age is almost here.

title: We Are Type I
narratorText: We have learned to draw on our star at a planetary scale, and the future now feels like something we can build rather than merely survive. The Dyson array grows daily. Our ambitions grow faster.

title: The First Era
narratorText: We are no longer a species clinging to one wounded cradle. We have made multiple worlds habitable and kept going. The first era of interplanetary civilisation is complete. What comes next is unwritten.

title: We Are Type II
narratorText: We have wrapped our industry around the Sun so completely that the scale of our work is no longer planetary — it is stellar. The Dyson sphere is complete. We are the light-weavers now.

title: Into the Dark Between Stars
narratorText: We have not become a galactic civilisation — not yet. But we have sent a living promise beyond the Sun and into the dark between stars. Whatever finds it one day will know we were here.

title: Ten Percent
narratorText: One in ten of the Sun's rays now strikes a panel we built. From here, the growth compounds. We can feel the energy budget of the solar system beginning to tip in our favour.

title: A Quarter of the Sun
narratorText: A quarter of the Sun's output now passes through our hands before it reaches the inner planets. This is not just energy. This is control of a star.

title: Halfway to the Stars
narratorText: We have passed the halfway point. The Sun is half-wrapped in our ambition. Fifty years ago this would have been mythology. Now it is an infrastructure report.

title: The First Ocean
narratorText: We have confirmed it. Standing water — persistent, open, real — on the surface of Mars. Every biologist we have is weeping. We understand why.

title: The First Birthday
narratorText: Someone on the lunar base turned thirty today — for the first time in their life, away from Earth. There was a cake. They used powdered ingredients and a microwave-adapted heating element. It was, by most accounts, terrible. Everyone ate two slices.

title: Those Who Will Not Come Home
narratorText: Three crew members have formally requested to remain on the lunar base permanently. They are healthy, productive, and clear-eyed about what they are choosing. They say Earth no longer feels like home. Do we honour that?

choices.label: Honour their choice. They know what they are.
Recall them. The mission needs rotation discipline.

title: The Great Silence
narratorText: Our long-range signal arrays have mapped a significant portion of nearby stellar neighbourhood. Nothing. Not a flicker of intentional radio, not a structured pulse, nothing that looks built. We are alone — or the silence is a warning. What do we do with this?

choices.label: Broadcast our existence. Let anyone who is listening know we are here.
Go quiet. The silence may be a survival strategy.
Continue as normal. The silence changes nothing.

title: The Mirror Goes Up
narratorText: The first orbital shade mirror is in position. Venus, for the first time in four billion years, is receiving less sunlight than yesterday. We are turning down the heat of a planet. The atmosphere will take centuries to respond. We are patient.

title: The Long Explosion
narratorText: We have detonated the polar ice cap charges. The shockwave is visible from orbit. The CO2 sublimation curve will run for decades. It is not subtle — but it is fast. The naturalists are already drafting their objections. They are not wrong. We did it anyway.

title: Something Stirs in the Ice
narratorText: Deep-range sonar from the Europa monitoring stations has detected structured pressure waves in the sub-ice ocean. They are not geological. We do not know what they are. We have decided, for now, not to broadcast this.

title: We Redirected Europa
narratorText: The vote passed by eleven. We have fired the mass drivers and committed Europa to a Venus intercept trajectory. Whatever was in the ocean is now our responsibility. Some things cannot be undone. We voted for this one anyway.

title: The Atmosphere Stirs
narratorText: The instruments do not lie, but they still surprise us. Carbon dioxide concentrations on Mars are measurably higher than when we arrived. The pressure curve is tiny — fractions of a millibar — but it is rising. We are doing this. We are actually doing this.

title: First Frost, First Rain
narratorText: A field team at the northern basin has reported something the geologists are calling provisional precipitation — water ice, cycling through a short melt each morning and refreezing by noon. It is not a river. It is barely a puddle. We are celebrating anyway.

title: The Red Gives Way
narratorText: The orbital imaging team has flagged something in the equatorial highlands: a faint but consistent green signature, spreading slowly outward from the seeded zones. We have been staring at the images for hours. Mars is growing something. We planted it, and it is growing.

title: The Long Shadow
narratorText: The shade mirror array is fully operational and Venus is, for the first time in four billion years, receiving less solar energy than the day before. The atmosphere will take generations to respond. We are not building this for ourselves. We are building it for whoever comes next.

title: The Skies Thin
narratorText: Spectroscopic data confirms what we hoped: the upper sulphuric acid cloud layer has thinned by forty percent. For the first time since we began, an observer on the surface could theoretically see a dim outline of the sun through the overcast. The surface is still lethal. But the sky is changing.

title: A Second Spring
narratorText: Surface thermometers are reading below sixty degrees Celsius for the first time. The pressure is still three atmospheres — no one is breathing this yet — but the models now show a stable equilibrium approaching. We may live to see this planet become something a person could stand on without dying. That is not a small thing.

title: The First Organisms Take Hold
narratorText: The microbial census is in. The pioneer organisms are not just surviving — they are spreading, metabolising, and dying in the right cycles. Mars has never hosted a carbon cycle before. It does now. We are its first biologists and its first gardeners.

title: The Soil Remembers
narratorText: Nitrogen is fixing. The chemolithotrophs have built a mineral scaffold the plants will one day inherit. Decades of work and the reward is a thin crust of chemistry that a child could scoop up in a handful. We are proud of every gram of it.

title: Green on Red
narratorText: The orbital imaging team flagged it at 0400 and no one went back to sleep. A faint green signature in the equatorial lowlands, spreading slowly outward from the seeded zones. Mars is growing something. We planted it, and it is growing.

title: A Biosphere Confirmed
narratorText: The stability models have converged. Mars now maintains a self-reinforcing biological system — organisms decomposing, cycling, reproducing, competing. We did not just seed it. We handed it off. Whatever grows here from now on will not need us to keep it alive.

title: The Acid Recedes
narratorText: Sulphuric acid concentrations in the lower atmosphere have dropped below the threshold we set as our first target. The sulphur-reducers did what nothing natural ever managed on this planet. The ocean floor is less hostile today than it was a decade ago. We start again from here.

title: An Ocean Stirs
narratorText: The aquatic layer is establishing. Phytoplankton analogues are producing oxygen in quantities the sensors can measure. We have built an ocean from chemistry, patience, and organisms smaller than a human cell. It is the strangest beautiful thing we have ever done.

title: The Atmosphere Answers
narratorText: Nitrogen is stabilising. Coastal mosses are filtering air through root mats that, three generations from now, someone might walk over without a suit. We are not done. But we have crossed the line from possible to underway.

title: The Second World Breathes
narratorText: The biosphere model has closed. Venus sustains a complete ecological loop — producers, consumers, decomposers, cycling nutrients through soil and water and air. We started with acid rain and a runaway greenhouse. We are ending with a world that breathes. We will allow ourselves, just this once, to call it a miracle.
